g.data-narration: Welcome to Smart Contracts in Blockchain - self-executing digital agreements
A smart contract is a self-executing program stored on blockchain that automatically enforces agreed terms when predefined conditions are met
g.text: A smart contract is a self-executing program stored on blockchain that automatically
enforces agreed terms when predefined conditions are met. It functions as a digital
contract that eliminates intermediaries and ensures trustless execution.

g.data-narration: Smart contracts have five key characteristics: automatic execution, trustless nature, transparency, immutability, and deterministic behavior
The execution flow involves five steps: event trigger, condition validation, contract execution, network consensus, and state update
g.text: 3. How Smart Contracts Work: The Execution Flow

g.data-narration: The lifecycle has six phases: develop, deploy, invoke, execute, record, and terminate. Contracts can also migrate to new versions
Traditional contracts require intermediaries and manual enforcement while smart contracts execute automatically with code as law
g.text: Code is Law - Contracts are immutable and self-executing once deployed

g.data-narration: Smart contracts operate in three layers: application layer for user interfaces, contract layer for business logic, and consensus layer for blockchain validation
g.text: Contract Layer (Business Logic - Smart Contract Code)

g.data-narration: Smart contracts enable DeFi for lending and trading, supply chain tracking, and transparent voting systems in real-world applications
Additional applications include NFTs for digital ownership, real estate for automated property transfers, healthcare for records, and identity management
Major vulnerabilities include reentrancy attacks, integer overflow, and access control issues that have led to significant financial losses
Other risks include front-running, untrusted external calls, denial of service attacks, and short address attacks that exploit smart contract weaknesses
Best practices include using checks-effects-interactions pattern, SafeMath library, proper access control, and comprehensive testing including formal verification
g.text: Update state before making external calls to prevent reentrancy
Prevents integer overflow and underflow by checking arithmetic bounds
Unit tests, integration tests, formal verification

g.data-narration: Design principles include KISS (Keep It Simple), DRY (Do not Repeat Code), modularity, and failing securely by defaulting to safe states
g.text: Default to safe state on error

g.data-narration: Key takeaways include smart contracts being self-executing and trustless, immutable once deployed, with multiple real-world applications requiring careful security design
Smart contracts are revolutionizing trustless transactions across industries - continue learning to build secure and efficient smart contracts
g.text: Smart contracts are revolutionizing trustless transactions across industries
Continue learning to build secure and efficient smart contracts
